nativeRange.habitat: Shallow lagoons and subtidal reef flats, generally found at depths of 0–20 m, closely associated with branching coral thickets (especially Acropora and Pocillopora) for refuge.

decorPreferences: open swimming space above the rockwork

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on zooplankton, benthic invertebrates and algae. In the aquarium tends to readily take a varied diet of frozen meaty foods (mysis, brine), pellets/flakes, and herbivore preparations; small frequent meals are generally advised over a single large feeding.

imageCandidates.notes: Leonard Low; 1664x1232. Group of six humbug damsels shoaling over branching coral - best represents this shoaling species as it is kept/displayed; all fish clearly show the three black bars and white tail; foreground fish sharp (slight underwater haze on background fish).

Rickard Zerpe; 2345x1759. Tack-sharp single-fish close-up, whole animal in frame; crisp diagnostic detail of the three black bars on a bright white body and the translucent white caudal fin (whitetail dascyllus).

Paul Asman and Jill Lenoble; 2785x1857. Wide in-situ reef shot with several humbug damsels around a coral bommie, showing the branching-coral association from the habitat notes; fish are smaller in frame so kept as a non-recommended context shot.

summary: The Three Stripe Damselfish (Dascyllus aruanus) is a hardy black-and-white striped Indo-Pacific species well suited to beginners, though it tends to become highly territorial as it matures and is best added last to a tank stocked with robust tankmates.
careNotes: The Three Stripe Damselfish is commonly considered one of the easiest marine fish to start with, but it has a strong reputation for growing into a highly territorial adult that will harass smaller or more peaceful tankmates. It is best kept either singly or in a group of 4–8 in a sufficiently large tank so that dominance aggression is spread across the group rather than focused on a single fish. Adding it to an established tank last, after calmer tankmates are already settled, is generally advised. Dense rockwork or branching coral structures for refuge, combined with open swimming space above, are strongly recommended. The species is generally considered reef-safe — it does not nip corals and tends to ignore most invertebrates — though its territorial nature can stress more sensitive reef inhabitants.
breedingNotes: The Three Stripe Damselfish has been bred in captivity, though it is not widely pursued as a breeding project. It is an oviparous demersal spawner, meaning the eggs are laid on and attached to a hard surface rather than scattered into the water column. Males clear and guard a nest site on hard substrate, court passing females, and tend the eggs until pelagic larvae emerge, generally within 3–5 days. Rearing the larvae requires live first-foods — rotifers initially, progressing to Artemia — and is generally considered moderate in difficulty.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Aquarium-trade care sheet: 30 gal minimum, easy care level, omnivore diet, temp 72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12, adult size ~3.75 in.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Pomacentridae), max length (10 cm TL), distribution (Indo-West Pacific), wild thermal range (24.9–29.3°C), max reported age (6 yr), and reproductive biology.

additional.name: Reef2Reef
additional.notes: Aquarist-community context corroborating the highly territorial adult temperament and the recommendation to keep either singly or in a group of 4–8 to dilute aggression. Used to bias the minVolumeLiters above the LiveAquaria 30-gal floor toward 150 L given the prose cues (territorial, needs open swimming space + rockwork).

fish.breedingNotes: Males clear and guard a nest site on hard substrate. Larvae are pelagic and require live first-foods — rotifers progressing to Artemia — for successful rearing.
